product_name: Vitamin D3 3000 IU Natural Blueberry Flavor

ingredients.name: Vitamin D
ingredients.notes: The gold standard form of vitamin D — identical to what your skin produces from sunlight. D3 raises and maintains blood 25(OH)D levels significantly better than D2. The most studied and recommended form by clinical guidelines worldwide.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D does not directly affect platelet aggregation at physiological or supplemental doses. However, high-dose vitamin D supplementation may interact with warfarin sensitivity in patients with concurrent vitamin K deficiency by affecting the vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X). The vitamin D endocrine system and vitamin K share overlapping roles in gamma-carboxylation of hepatic Gla-proteins.
condition_hits.action: Vitamin D at standard supplemental doses (up to 4000 IU/day) is not a clinically significant bleeding risk on its own. Monitor if taking high-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) with anticoagulants.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D exerts broad immunomodulatory effects, generally shifting immune responses toward tolerance via Treg cell upregulation and Th17 suppression. Low vitamin D status is associated with increased autoimmune risk. Supplementation may be beneficial in Hashimoto thyroiditis and multiple sclerosis, but high doses in active autoimmune disease require monitoring.
condition_hits.action: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and moderate supplementation is generally considered beneficial. However, pharmacologic doses (>4000 IU/day chronically) can cause hypercalcemia, which promotes vascular calcification and may worsen coronary artery disease. The benefit-risk ratio is dose-dependent.
condition_hits.action: Target serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–60 ng/mL. Avoid high-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) without clinical monitoring of calcium and 25(OH)D levels.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D receptors (VDR) are expressed in ovarian granulosa cells, endometrium, and placenta. A 2017 review (Voulgaris et al., Hormones) summarized strong associations between vitamin D status and PCOS metabolic parameters, endometriosis, and IVF outcomes, with deficiency linked to lower clinical pregnancy rates. Meta-analyses of IVF studies suggest women with 25(OH)D >20 ng/mL have significantly better outcomes, though causality is not fully established.
condition_hits.action: Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.
condition_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin D with your clinician.
condition_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: High-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) has been associated with altered warfarin sensitivity in some patients. The proposed mechanism involves shared metabolic pathways with vitamin K: vitamin D may modulate hepatic expression of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X), and vitamin D deficiency itself affects coagulation factor synthesis. Vitamin D and K have documented synergistic effects on Gla-protein carboxylation, and disruption of this balance at supraphysiological vitamin D doses may affect INR stability in warfarin patients.
drug_class_hits.action: Monitor INR stability when significantly changing vitamin D supplementation dose, particularly at high doses (>10,000 IU/day). Routine supplementation doses (1000-4000 IU/day) typically do not require INR monitoring changes.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: No established direct interaction between vitamin D supplementation and antiplatelet drug activity. Monitoring recommended at high doses due to broader hemostatic pathway involvement.
drug_class_hits.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses. Monitor for unusual bleeding if taking high-dose vitamin D with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: No established pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between vitamin D and NSAIDs on platelet or coagulation pathways. Theoretical concern at high doses given shared anti-inflammatory regulatory roles.
drug_class_hits.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses.
drug_class_hits.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Vitamin D has additive immunomodulatory effects that may complement immunosuppressant therapy. Standard-dose supplementation is generally considered safe alongside immunosuppressants and may reduce corticosteroid-induced bone loss.
drug_class_hits.action: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

ingredients.name: Calcium
ingredients.notes: Calcium combined with phosphate — provides both minerals. Poor solubility limits absorption (~25%). Commonly used as a tablet filler and binder rather than a primary calcium source. Tricalcium phosphate (TCP) is the most common form.

condition_hits.mechanism: Calcium supplements (not dietary calcium) are associated with a modestly increased risk of myocardial infarction. Bolland et al. WHI reanalysis found RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.07–1.45) for MI with supplemental calcium. Proposed mechanism: acute hypercalcemia following bolus supplementation may promote coronary arterial calcification and thrombosis. Risk may be attenuated by co-administration with vitamin D.
condition_hits.action: In individuals with existing heart disease, prefer dietary calcium over supplements. If supplementation is necessary, use the lowest effective dose and split doses to blunt calcium spikes. Discuss with cardiologist.
condition_hits.alert_body: Calcium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding calcium.
condition_hits.informational_note: Calcium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_hits.mechanism: Calcium forms insoluble chelate complexes with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing oral absorption by 20–25%. Effect is dose-dependent and clinically significant at standard supplementation doses (600–1000 mg/day).
drug_class_hits.action: Separate calcium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours to prevent absorption interference.
drug_class_hits.alert_body: Calcium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing calcium away from your dose.
drug_class_hits.informational_note: Calcium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

inactive_ingredients.name: Honey
inactive_ingredients.notes: Natural sweetener produced by bees from flower nectar. Used as a sweetener, binder, or flavor in supplements. Generally safe but can cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals. Not recommended for infants under 1 year.

inactive_ingredients.name: Sugar
inactive_ingredients.notes: Natural sugars used for sweetening and palatability. Generally recognized as safe but should be consumed in moderation.

inactive_ingredients.name: Pear juice concentrate
inactive_ingredients.notes: Natural fruit juice concentrates used for color and flavor. Clean label ingredients. GRAS approved.

inactive_ingredients.name: Gelatin
inactive_ingredients.notes: Derived from animal collagen, forming the shell of traditional capsules and softgels. Provides a protective casing. While animal-derived (not vegan/vegetarian), it is generally considered safe and inert in its role as a capsule material.

inactive_ingredients.name: Water
inactive_ingredients.notes: A fundamental solvent and diluent used in liquid formulations, suspensions, and as a component in the manufacturing of capsules and tablets. Considered entirely inert and safe.

inactive_ingredients.name: Citric Acid
inactive_ingredients.notes: Natural preservative and pH adjuster. While naturally found in citrus, commercial form is typically derived from fermented sources. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS). Clean label preservative option.

inactive_ingredients.name: Colors
inactive_ingredients.notes: Low-severity flag for unspecified color listings. Penalize lightly unless explicit dye tokens indicate artificial colorants.
inactive_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Lack of disclosure: color source is not specified, which reduces transparency and can mask artificial dye use.
population_warnings: Dye-sensitive individuals — cannot assess risk without knowing which colorant is used
Children — if undisclosed colorant is an azo dye, hyperactivity concern applies
inactive_ingredients.harmful_notes: Lack of disclosure: color source is not specified, which reduces transparency and can mask artificial dye use.

inactive_ingredients.name: Fumaric Acid
inactive_ingredients.notes: Acidulant used for tartness and pH control in gummies/tablets. Common food-grade additive; treated as non-scorable formulation excipient.

inactive_ingredients.name: Lactic Acid
inactive_ingredients.notes: Organic acid used as pH adjuster and preservative in supplement formulations. Naturally occurring in fermented foods and muscle tissue. Generally recognized as safe.

inactive_ingredients.name: Natural flavor
inactive_ingredients.notes: Flavoring substances derived from plant or animal sources. While generally recognized as safe, the term can encompass hundreds of compounds. Must be derived from natural sources per FDA regulations. Good clean label choice.

inactive_ingredients.name: Pectin
inactive_ingredients.notes: Natural fiber from fruits, excellent for gelling and thickening. Provides additional health benefits including cholesterol support.

inactive_ingredients.name: Sodium Citrate
inactive_ingredients.notes: FDA GRAS-approved buffer and acidity regulator widely used safely in foods and supplements. Helps stabilize pH and acts as natural preservative. Consolidates two previous duplicate entries (Sodium Citrate and Trisodium Citrate - they are the same compound). Only consideration is moderate sodium content for sodium-restricted diets.

warnings.title: Contains Unspecified Colors
warnings.detail: Lack of disclosure: color source is not specified, which reduces transparency and can mask artificial dye use.
warnings.notes: Low-severity flag for unspecified color listings. Penalize lightly unless explicit dye tokens indicate artificial colorants.
warnings.mechanism_of_harm: Lack of disclosure: color source is not specified, which reduces transparency and can mask artificial dye use.
population_warnings: Dye-sensitive individuals — cannot assess risk without knowing which colorant is used
Children — if undisclosed colorant is an azo dye, hyperactivity concern applies
warnings.safety_summary: The label does not specify the color source, which limits transparency and can mask artificial dye use. Worth choosing products that disclose the specific colorant.

clinical_matches.effect_direction_rationale: Retained positive_strong classification; study_type=systematic_review_meta; evidence_level=ingredient-human; primary_outcome=Immune Support; published_tags=RCT,meta-analysis,systematic_review; registry_completed_trials_count=2067; total_enrollment=576; endpoint_tags=immune_support; notes/citations contain no automated downgrade signals.
clinical_matches.notes: Extensive evidence from systematic reviews and meta-analyses (2024-2025) supports vitamin D3 for bone health, immune function, and mood. 2024 Endocrine Society guidelines recommend empiric supplementation for specific populations.
clinical_matches.notable_studies: Bolland et al. (2018, PMID 30293909) systematic review/meta-analysis of 81 RCTs found that vitamin D did not prevent fractures or falls or meaningfully improve BMD. Martineau et al. (2017, PMID 28202713) individual-participant-data meta-analysis found vitamin D reduced acute respiratory infections overall, with the clearest benefit in deficient individuals.

references_structured.title: Effects of vitamin D supplementation on musculoskeletal health: a systematic review, meta-analysis, and trial sequential analysis.
Vitamin D supplementation to prevent acute respiratory tract infections: systematic review and meta-analysis of individual participant data.

clinical_matches.effect_direction_rationale: Retained positive_strong classification; study_type=systematic_review_meta; evidence_level=ingredient-human; primary_outcome=Joint & Bone Health; published_tags=RCT,meta-analysis,systematic_review; registry_completed_trials_count=3121; total_enrollment=36282; endpoint_tags=cardiovascular; notes/citations contain no automated downgrade signals.
clinical_matches.notes: Broad human evidence base in bone-health settings, especially when paired with vitamin D and adequate baseline intake assessment.
clinical_matches.notable_studies: Updated pooled evidence (PMID 26510847) supports calcium plus vitamin D for fracture-risk reduction in selected older or at-risk populations, while earlier meta-analytic work (PMID 17720017) also found support for fracture and bone-loss prevention in adults over 50 when intake and adherence are adequate.

references_structured.title: Calcium plus vitamin D supplementation and risk of fractures: an updated meta-analysis from the National Osteoporosis Foundation.
references_structured.citation: Osteoporosis international : a journal established as result of cooperation between the European Foundation for Osteoporosis and the National Osteoporosis Foundation of the USA (2016-01-01)

references_structured.title: Use of calcium or calcium in combination with vitamin D supplementation to prevent fractures and bone loss in people aged 50 years and older: a meta-analysis.

synergy_detail.bonus_explanation: This product combines co-dependent nutrients that work together in established biochemical pathways.

clusters.name: Immune Defense
clusters.mechanism: Vitamin C supports neutrophil function and acts as a cofactor for immune signaling enzymes. Zinc is required for T-cell maturation and NK cell activity. Vitamin D modulates innate immune responses through antimicrobial peptide (cathelicidin) expression. Quercetin has demonstrated ionophore activity
clusters.benefit_short: Vitamin C, zinc, vitamin D, and elderberry work on different immune pathways — giving your defense system multiple angles at once.

clusters.name: Hormone Balance (Men)
clusters.mechanism: Zinc is essential for testosterone synthesis and 5-alpha-reductase regulation. Ashwagandha supports testosterone via cortisol reduction (inverse relationship). Vitamin D acts as a steroid hormone precursor. Tongkat ali may support free testosterone by reducing SHBG binding.
clusters.benefit_short: Zinc, ashwagandha, vitamin D, and tongkat ali support healthy testosterone pathways — a common stack for men's hormonal balance.

clusters.name: Bone Health
clusters.mechanism: Calcium provides building blocks; Vitamin D enhances absorption; K2 (MK-7) activates osteocalcin to deposit calcium in bones; Magnesium is cofactor for vitamin D activation.
clusters.benefit_short: Calcium, vitamin D, K2, magnesium, and boron work together for bone strength — calcium alone isn't enough, these partners guide it to bone.

clusters.name: Kidney Health Support
clusters.mechanism: Astragalus and cordyceps have nephroprotective properties. Omega-3s reduce inflammation. CoQ10 supports mitochondrial function in kidney cells. Alpha-lipoic acid provides antioxidant protection.
clusters.benefit_short: Astragalus, cordyceps, alpha-lipoic acid, and omega-3 together support kidney function — traditional herbs plus modern antioxidants.

clusters.name: Dental & Oral Health
clusters.mechanism: CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy in gingival fibroblasts and has demonstrated benefit in periodontal inflammation. Vitamin D enhances calcium absorption for enamel remineralization and modulates oral immune defenses. Vitamin C is critical for collagen synthesis in gingival connective tissue. Zinc
clusters.benefit_short: CoQ10, vitamin D, K2, and probiotics together support gum and tooth health from the inside — beyond just brushing and flossing.

clusters.name: Migraine Prevention
clusters.mechanism: Magnesium blocks cortical spreading depression (the neurophysiological substrate of migraine aura) and modulates NMDA receptors. Riboflavin (B2) at high dose improves mitochondrial energy metabolism in neurons with impaired oxidative phosphorylation. CoQ10 supports mitochondrial electron transport i
clusters.benefit_short: Magnesium, riboflavin (B2), CoQ10, and feverfew are the most-studied supplement combo for reducing migraine frequency — the headache stack.

clusters.name: Fertility (Female)
clusters.mechanism: Folate (as 5-MTHF) is critical for DNA methylation during oocyte maturation and early embryogenesis. CoQ10 supports mitochondrial energy in oocytes, which declines with age and correlates with egg quality. Myo-inositol acts as a second messenger in insulin signaling and improves oocyte quality in PC
clusters.benefit_short: Methylfolate, CoQ10, inositol, vitamin D, and omega-3 together support egg quality and cycle regularity — a common fertility-preparation stack.
